trajectory_summary: The call opens with a stale but cooperative lead and ends in a self-inflicted DQ two minutes later. Holland identifies shade from satellite imagery, declares the home unworkable without asking a single qualifying question about tree removal plans or bill pain, and releases the lead with a passive callback offer. The customer was never given the opportunity to re-qualify himself.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown' — based on the transcript, the correct tag is 'disqualified / lead released.' However, the DQ itself is procedurally questionable: Holland reached a DQ conclusion from satellite data alone without completing any Verify items (bill, interest, roof, title) and without asking the single question that could have preserved the lead (tree removal plans). A more accurate outcome tag would be 'premature_DQ' or 'rep-initiated release without full qualification.'

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Premature DQ — missing the tree-removal probe
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I do think that you have too much shade for solar to be beneficial.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland ends a cooperative, workable lead in under two minutes by declaring a site-assessment conclusion without asking the one question that could have preserved it. The manager should open the 1-on-1 with: 'Walk me through your process when you see shade on satellite.' The answer will reveal whether Holland is freelancing a DQ the process doesn't authorize, or following a workflow that has a gap in it. Either way, the coaching fix is a single question: 'Do you have any plans to remove those trees?' — asked before any DQ conclusion is stated.

key_moments.quote: That was quite a while ago. We haven't haven't talked about it for about six months.
key_moments.stewart_read: Roger's stale-lead disclosure is a textbook flip opportunity. The six-month gap is not a disqualifier — it is an opening. A rep executing the objection_inversion move would pivot immediately: 'Has your bill changed in the last six months?' or 'A lot of people who looked into it six months ago are coming back now because their summer bills hit.' Holland instead responds with a generic free-estimate pitch, treating the gap as neutral context rather than as leverage. Reactive read holds: no evidence Holland was setting up a later move — the pitch that follows is generic, not gap-specific.

key_moments.quote: they looked at things, but, I think they took some measurements or pictures or something, towards the east, but they never got back to us.
key_moments.stewart_read: Roger is disclosing that a prior solar company came to his home, took measurements, and then ghosted him. This is a trust-damage signal — he was left hanging by a competitor. It is also a verify.prior_designs hit AND a micro-trade opportunity: Holland could have acknowledged the bad experience explicitly ('That's frustrating — you gave them your time and they disappeared') and then used it as a legitimacy bridge ('That's exactly why we do things differently — our specialist follows up with a full design'). Instead, Holland responds with 'Oh, okay' and pivots immediately to the free estimate pitch. The empathy moment is skipped entirely.

key_moments.quote: What are your company Well, there's no no charge for that. Right? Not just Yeah. No. It you're not committed to anything.
key_moments.stewart_read: Roger begins to ask a question about the company — the transcript captures 'What are your company' before the line breaks. Holland cuts across it with the no-charge / no-commitment reassurance before Roger can finish. This is a missed Verify entry point: Roger was about to ask something that would have surfaced either a legitimacy concern or a competitive question. Holland's interruption forecloses the answer before the question is complete. The no-commitment reassurance is not wrong, but it is premature — Holland doesn't know what Roger was actually asking.

key_moments.quote: yeah, I see that you have a couple of pretty large trees next to you. Are those trees still there?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland's satellite-based tree check is the pivot point of the entire call. The question itself is not wrong — shading is a legitimate site factor. The failure is in what Holland does with the answer. Roger confirms the trees are present, and Holland immediately concludes DQ without asking: (a) whether Roger has plans to remove them, (b) what Roger's electric bill looks like, (c) whether the roof faces other directions, or (d) any other Verify item. The DQ is reached from a single satellite observation and a one-word customer confirmation. This is scope creep into site-assessment territory that belongs to the closer, combined with a premature DQ that bypasses the full qualification process. Strategic read: there is a plausible deliberate-filter reading — Holland may be running a satellite-first triage to avoid wasting closer time on unworkable sites. But even under that reading, the correct triage question is 'Do you have plans to remove those trees?' not a unilateral DQ declaration.
